title: About
text:
Hi, I’m Tiara, a data generalist with 10 years of working experience with data.
I started out as a data science educator in Jakarta 🇮🇩. Now, I build analytics solutions for D2C brands @ Leaf Grow from Newcastle upon Tyne 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿.
I mainly work in R, Python, SQL, a bit of statistics, and a whole lot of common sense. I wandered my way through data science, BI, analytics, and data engineering and these days, I’ve accepted my fate as a data generalist.

title: About
text:
Core Stack

Analytics Engineering, building well-grounded data semantic layers that don’t require everyone to @ me to pull data themselves.
Business Intelligence, actively checking dashboard activity and calling users out for not actually needing an “urgent” dashboard fix.
Data Warehousing, currently in an unhealthy relationship with Snowflake usage alerts thinking I can fix it (Snowflake Compute Cost).
Data Strategy, eliciting, strategising, and planning out team efforts so that I can rub my hands instead of stressing every time executives ask, “why are we not spending time on the other stuff instead?”

Started documenting my work so future me can thank present me. On good days, I also post my thoughts along the way.

title: Hello, Python
text: This post is a .qmd file with Python code chunks. Quarto runs the code at render time and inlines the output (numbers, tables, plots) into the page. To start a new post, copy this folder and edit index.qmd.

title: Hello, Python
text:
A tiny dataset

import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "month": pd.date_range("2026-01-01", periods=6, freq="MS"),
    "revenue": [120, 138, 155, 162, 180, 205],
    "channel": ["organic"] * 6,
})
df

title: Hello, Python
text:
A quick plot

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3.5))
ax.plot(df["month"], df["revenue"], marker="o")
ax.set_title("Monthly revenue, organic channel")
ax.set_ylabel("Revenue ($k)")
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Hello, Python
text:
Notes

Use jupyter: python3 in the YAML header to enable Python execution.
Heavy outputs (model training, big queries) get cached on first render via freeze: auto in _quarto.yml — only the chunks you change get re-executed.

title: Hello, Quarto
text:
Markdown is an easy to read and write text format:

It’s plain text so works well with version control
It can be rendered into HTML, PDF, and more
Learn more at: https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/

title: Hello, R
text: This post is an .Rmd file rendered by Quarto. Same chunk syntax you know from Rmarkdown — Quarto picks up the knitr engine automatically when it sees R chunks.

title: Hello, R
text:
A tiny dataset

library(tibble)
library(dplyr)

sales <- tibble(
  month   = seq(as.Date("2026-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 6),
  revenue = c(120, 138, 155, 162, 180, 205),
  channel = "organic"
)
sales

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  month      revenue channel
  <date>       <dbl> <chr>  
1 2026-01-01     120 organic
2 2026-02-01     138 organic
3 2026-03-01     155 organic
4 2026-04-01     162 organic
5 2026-05-01     180 organic
6 2026-06-01     205 organic

title: Hello, R
text:
A quick plot

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(sales, aes(month, revenue)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Monthly revenue, organic channel",
       x = NULL, y = "Revenue ($k)") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hello, R
text:
Notes

Quarto renders .Rmd files the same way it renders .qmd files with R chunks. If you prefer the newer Quarto syntax, just rename the file to index.qmd.
Use chunk options as YAML-style #| comments (Quarto convention), or the classic inline {r, fig.width=6} form — both work.
